coredata.dc:description: Solid electrolytes have received widespread attention due to their higher safety than liquid electrolytes in the past decades. In particular, organic-inorganic composite solid electrolytes (CSEs) in which inorganic fillers dispersed in polymer solid electrolytes are considered to be one of the most promising candidate electrolytes for high-performance solid-state lithium batteries. Understanding the local environments and the conduction pathway/dynamics of Li+ is essential for the design of high-performance CSEs. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is a non-invasive quantitative technique that has unique capabilities in providing molecular structure information, morphological evolution, and measuring the movement of ions at different time scales. Therefore, for battery researchers, an accurate and comprehensive understanding of the basic principles and experimental design of solid-state NMR (SSNMR) is of great significance for investigating the abundant molecular structure and dynamics information in CSEs. The specific applications of the SSNMR technique in CSEs are briefly introduced in this present review.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid electrolytes have received widespread attention due to their higher safety than liquid electrolytes in the past decades. In particular, organic-inorganic composite solid electrolytes (CSEs) in which inorganic fillers dispersed in polymer solid electrolytes are considered to be one of the most promising candidate electrolytes for high-performance solid-state lithium batteries. Understanding the local environments and the conduction pathway/dynamics of Li+ is essential for the design of high-performance CSEs. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is a non-invasive quantitative technique that has unique capabilities in providing molecular structure information, morphological evolution, and measuring the movement of ions at different time scales. Therefore, for battery researchers, an accurate and comprehensive understanding of the basic principles and experimental design of solid-state NMR (SSNMR) is of great significance for investigating the abundant molecular structure and dynamics information in CSEs. The specific applications of the SSNMR technique in CSEs are briefly introduced in this present review.

Schematic diagram of characterizing material structure, ion dynamics and ion conduction pathway by SSNMR techniques.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely applied in portable devices, electric vehicles, and large-scale energy storage due to high energy density, good cycling stability, and high operating voltage [1–4]. However, a series of safety issues such as the use of flammable organic liquid electrolytes and the short circuit caused by the formation of lithium dendrites during overcharge or abused operations have aroused widespread public concerns. All-solid-state batteries, in which the traditional organic liquid electrolytes are substituted with solid-state electrolytes (SSEs), are believed to possess better safety performance. Not only solves the safety issues of LIBs also allows using metal lithium anode to replace the graphite anode in traditional LIBs to achieve higher energy density. Therefore, developing solid electrolytes with high ionic conductivity, good chemical and electrochemical stability, and excellent mechanical properties are essential to all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries.

Currently, three types of SSEs are widely under investigation: solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs), inorganic ceramic electrolytes (ICEs), and organic-inorganic composite solid electrolytes (CSEs). Polymer electrolytes with high flexibility, processability, and good adhesion to electrodes have attracted wide attention in rechargeable batteries. Popular polymer electrolytes include poly (ethylene oxide) (PEO), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), and their derivatives [5–10]. These polymer systems have significant advantages in promoting the dissolution of lithium salt, however, the ion conductivity of most SPE is below 10−5 S/cm at room temperature (RT), making it impossible to meet the needs of real rechargeable battery applications. Unlike SPE, ICEs typically have a high ion conductivity (10−4-10−2 S/cm), large ion transfer number (TLi +∼1) wide electrochemical stability window as well as good stability against Li metal anode. However, ICEs are fragile and show large interface resistances in contact with electrodes, which compromise the cycle stability and the rate performance of solid-state batteries. To balance the pros and cons of the two electrolyte families, preparing organic-inorganic CSEs by dispersing inorganic ceramic particles in polymer electrolytes is considered an effective strategy for obtaining high-performance solid electrolytes. The organic-inorganic CSEs are composed of a polymer matrix, inorganic inert ceramic fillers (e.g., ZrO2 [11], Al2O3 [12,13], TiO2 [14], SiO2 [5,15]), or active materials with ionic conductivity (e.g., LLZO (Li7La3Zr2O12) [16–18], LLTO (Li3La2/3-xTiO3) [19], Li3N [20,21], LAGP (Li1.5Al0.5Ge1.5(PO4)3) [22,23]. Compared with SPEs and ICEs, organic-inorganic CSEs possess well interfacial stability, excellent mechanical properties, and high ionic conductivity [24,25]. As a result, organic-inorganic CSEs are one of the most promising candidate electrolytes for improving solid-state battery performance. The interfacial layer between the inorganic filler and polymer could form a fast ion transport pathway through Lewis acid-base interaction in CSEs [26–29]. Although many experiments and computational simulations have been adopted to discuss the ion conduction behavior of the CSEs, the mechanism of Li+ ion conduction is not fully understood, such as the relationship between the formation of the internal interface layer of CSEs and polymers, inorganic fillers, and lithium salts. In addition to widely studied PEO-based electrolyte, the polymers such as polyethylene carbonate, polyvinyl carbonate, with Tg higher than PEO are mainly amorphous. And the ion conduction behavior of such polymers in CSEs is not clear.

Scientists have relied on various advanced characterization techniques (e.g., atomic absorption spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscope (SEM), Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), etc.) to characterize the structure, composition, and morphology of materials [30,31]. Among these characterization techniques, SSNMR is a non-destructive and highly selective technique, which mainly investigates the interactions between nuclei and the local environments [32–34]. Furthermore, the SSNMR technique has been demonstrated to be an indispensable characterization method investigating the ion diffusion kinetics in electrode materials and SSEs [35–38]. Firstly, SSNMR has high spectroscopic resolution and can simultaneously qualify and quantify the chemical compositions in the CSEs; secondly, SSNMR can provide key structural and conformational information at the atomic scale; thirdly, SSNMR can assess dynamic behaviors across a wide range of timescales, thus revealing the mechanisms of intermolecular interactions, chemical reactions and transport phenomena. In liquid-sate NMR, various orientation dependent interactions (e.g., chemical shift anisotropy, quadrupole interaction, and dipole-coupling interaction) can be averaged out by the rapid movement of molecules and obtain the high-resolution NMR spectra. SSNMR spectra are often broadened by these anisotropic interactions. Therefore, the main task of SSNMR spectroscopists is to extract critical chemical and structural information from solid samples by developing techniques for anisotropic characteristics. Through the continuous efforts and practice of NMR spectroscopists, advanced SSNMR techniques, including fast magic angle spinning (MAS), cross polarization (CP), and two-dimensional (2D) NMR to eliminate or reduce anisotropy and improve the resolution of the SSNMR spectrum. SSNMR methods are also powerful in detecting local or short-range or microscopic motions at different time scales. For instance, 2D exchange SSNMR spectroscopy is suitable for the slow motion of ions; line-shape analysis, spin-spin relaxation time (T 2) and spin-lattice relaxation in rotation of frame of reference (T 1ρ) are sensitive to motions at intermediate regime; and spin-lattice relaxation in laboratory frame of reference (T 1) is usually employed measuring ultra-rapid motions. Long-range ion transport or spin diffusion can be characterized by pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR.

Since some systematic reviews of CSEs have been published [39-42] the main purpose of this review is to summarize the recent research progress on the applications of SSNMR technology in CSEs. This article is organized into three parts: The first part focuses on the structural analysis of solid electrolytes, including the structural changes caused by chemical reactions or physical interactions between different components. The second part emphasizes SSNMR studies on understanding Li+ ions diffusion and the exchange mechanism. The third part is specifically dedicated to the research development of SSNMR on the ion migration pathways in organic-inorganic CSEs. Meanwhile, we have summarized the SSNMR techniques involved in each part of characterizing the material structure, ion dynamics, and ion conduction pathways on organic-inorganic CSEs in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure analysis of CSEs using SSNMR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The SSNMR technique is extremely sensitive to the local environment around the atom/nucleus under observation. Nuclei with different local environments can lead to a different chemical shift. Hence, SSNMR has aided in detecting the specific interactions between polymer chains and Li+ cations, the structure of organic components, and the behavior of mobile ionic substances [43,44]. In the liquid-state NMR spectrum, the anisotropic interactions in the molecules are eliminated owing to rapid isotropic molecular (or ionic) motion and leading to a very narrow resonance of characteristic line shape. Line broadening often occurs in SSNMR spectra due to the presence of angular dependent anisotropic interactions and once was thought as a hindrance. These spin interactions in SSNMR spectroscopy can provide valuable information on chemistry, structure, or dynamics. Line broadening can be solved by MAS combined with high-power decoupling (HPD) and/or CP, in which the magnetization vector of the abundant nuclei (usually protons) is transferred to the dilute nuclei (e.g., 29Si, 6Li, and 13C) through dipolar coupling, to eliminate chemical shift anisotropy and dipolar interactions to obtain the high-resolution spectrum. 6,7Li NMR is widely adopted to detect the local environments of Li+ in CSEs. Both 6Li and 7Li are NMR active isotopes. The natural abundances of 7Li and 6Li are about 92.6%, and 7.4%, respectively. Usually, a high resolution can be fulfilled in 6Li NMR owing to the weak dipolar coupling and small quadruple moment of the 6Li isotope. And Li+ located in different local structures (inorganic filler, polymer matrix, and inorganic/polymer interface) can be well distinguished. For example, Hu et al. measured the 6Li direct polarization with MAS (DP/MAS) of the PAN/LiClO4: LLZTO CSEs (Fig. 1 A) [45]. The 6Li signal due to Li+ at the interface of LLZTO/PAN (0.10 ppm) has been successfully distinguished from other signals of Li+ in bulk LLZTO (0.55 ppm) and LiClO4 at PAN/PAN interface (-0.48 ppm). Meanwhile, a continuous polymer/polymer interface as a fast ionic conduction path is created by electrospin/electrospray method, which provides opportunities for practical application of all-solid-state batteries. In most cases, the resolution of 7Li NMR is adequate. Chu et al. [46] employed 7Li MAS NMR to distinguish Li+ ions in different chemical environments or different phases in the Sm2O3-PEO/LiClO4 composite electrolytes (Fig. 1B). Compared with the typical 7Li NMR spectra of PEO/LiClO4, the 7Li MAS NMR of Sm2O3-PEO/LiClO4 has two shoulder peaks, which shifts and broaden with increasing Sm2O3 content. The appearance of shoulder peaks indicates that there is a coordination effect between Li+ and Sm2O3 oxide. Therefore, lithium ions exist in two environments in the composite electrolyte system, namely: (a) in the matrix of amorphous PEO; (b) in coordination with the oxygen atoms on the surface of Sm2O3.

In SSNMR studies of CSEs with inert inorganic fillers, identifying the interaction sites between polymer and the surface of filler is usually the major concern. For instance, Kao et al. [47] conducted solid-state 1H→13C CP MAS NMR and 29Si MAS NMR experiments to elucidate the structure of polymer chain and the condensed silicate filler in EGM-X (E = ED2003, G = GLYMO, M = MPEOPS, X = 32, 24, 8) organic-inorganic hybrid electrolytes. The condensation of organosilicon groups was monitored by 29Si MAS NMR, as shown in Fig. 1C. In the EGM-X hybrid electrolytes, there are two different 29Si signals located at −67.5 and −57.9 ppm, corresponding to the crosslinked T 3 site (R–SiO3, R = alkyl group) and the terminal T 2 site (R–SiO2(OH)), respectively. The observation of T 3 and T 2 signals confirms the presence of organosilicon groups in the organic-inorganic hybrid polymer electrolyte (Fig. 1C). With the salt concentration increases, the percentage of the crosslinked T 3 group (86%–80%) is higher than the terminal T 2 group (14%-20%), indicating a high degree of condensation, which according to the author may be owing to the presence of Li+ ions as acid catalysts. The synthesis route of polymer can be tracked using 1H→13C CP MAS NMR (Fig. 1D). The main peak at 70 ppm is the methylene carbon adjacent to the ether oxygen of the polymer chain, 16.7 ppm signal is the methyl carbon of the propylene oxide unit, and the peak at 49.0 ppm can be attributed to the nitrogen substituted carbon at the terminal of the diamines. There was a small peak at 54.5 ppm due to carbon in the epoxide ring of GLYMO, indicating that the opening of the epoxy ring was incomplete. The signal at 74.8 ppm can be assigned to the ether carbon in the polyether PPG segment. When the salt doping level increases, the peak becomes a broad shoulder peak and the resolution becomes worse. This is due to the high concentration of salt doping leading to a wide range distribution of the environments of polymer chains.

29Si MAS NMR is widely adopted in the identification of the degree of silicon condensation and the structure of the silica network inside the composite. However, different some time controversial conclusions about the key factor affecting the condensation process are reported by different authors. The 29Si MAS NMR spectra of CSEs based on diureasils doped with LiClO4 at different concentrations are shown in Fig. 1E [48]. There are two 29Si signals at -67 and -57 ppm, corresponding to T3 (RSi(OSi)3, R) alkyl group) and T2 (RSi(OSi)2(OH)) sites. Interestingly, the relative ratio changes of T3: T2 upon salt concentration indicate that the coagulation of the silicate network is more complete when the salt concentration is low. This suggests that the presence of LiClO4 interferes with the hydrolysis and the condensation process that form the silicate network, which is in some degree contradictory with the conclusion drawn in Ref. [46]. 1H→13C CP intensity build-up curve of 71 ppm signal due to ether carbon was measured as a function of contact time, as shown in Fig. 1F and G. The dynamics in the 1H→13C CP experiment are directly related to the rate of magnetization build-up between the H and C spins T CH and the T 1 of the proton in the rotating frame T1ρ(H). Generally speaking, the build-up process of the magnetization of 13C can be quantified using T CH , while the timescale of the decay of the magnetization is determined by T1ρ(H). The CP time constant TCH is governed by dipolar interactions, which can be weakened by motion. G(1)- U(2000)-8 (0.3 ms) has a slower build-up of spin magnetization for the peak at 71 ppm compared to G(1)- U(2000)-32 (1.4 ms), which corresponds to the slow motion of the latter makes the CP signal transfer from the proton spins more efficient than for the former. Fig. 1F indicated that with the increase of salt concentration, Li+ ions may coordinate with the ether oxygen atoms of the polymer chain, thereby limiting the movement of the chain segment and leading to a stronger 1H→13C dipole coupling effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the ions dynamics in CSEs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to structural analysis, the SSNMR technique is also suitable for investigating lithium diffusion parameters from macroscopic and microscopic perspectives. It can detect the jump rate and activation energy through many different technical means, including spin-spin and spin-lattice relaxation NMR, spin diffusion and/or chemical exchange, and variable temperature line-shape analysis.

The line-shape analysis of the 7Li/6Li NMR can easily distinguish fast-conducting Li+ from non-migrating Li+ in CSEs. Li et al. probed the Li+ environments in the composite polymer electrolytes (CPEs) doped with Mg(ClO4)2, CPE-0MC (0 wt% Mg(ClO4)2), and CPE-05MC (0.5 wt% Mg(ClO4)2), by 6Li SSNMR, as shown in Fig. 2 a [49]. The Gaussian peak at 0.82 ppm is attributed to the less mobile Li+ ions in the CPEs with higher coordination, and the Lorentzian peak at 0.72 ppm corresponds to the more mobile Li+ ions that are coordinated to EO and bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (TFSI−). Therefore, it can be concluded that introducing Mg2+ into the CPEs can enhance the number of mobile Li+ carriers in the CPE-05MC electrolyte. Sheng et al. [50] studied the influence of the Li1.3Al0.3Hf1.7(PO4)3 (LAHP) powders on the polymer electrolyte via 6Li SSNMR. The concentration of highly mobile Li+ increased from 30.6% to 60.0% by comparing the 6Li SSNMR spectra of pure PEO-LiTFSI and PEO-LiTFSI-20% LAHP systems, suggesting a strong interaction between LAHP and PEO electrolyte which increases the concentration of mobile Li+. Similar to CSEs, this phenomenon is more common in crystal-crystal composite materials [51–53]. For example, in the crystal-crystal composite materials, Li2O–Al2O3 and Li2O–B2O3 systems [34], the narrow and broad NMR lines correspond to the rapid Li+ conduction of in the interfacial regions and the slow Li+ diffusion in bulk Li2O, respectively (Fig. 2b). Thus, the shape of the NMR lines can directly reflect the heterogeneous structure of nanocrystalline material. The narrower the 7Li NMR signal, the faster the mobility of the 7Li nuclei in Li2O.

So far, most of the discussions consider the composite electrolytes as lithium single-ion conductors assuming the anions are fully attached to the polymer, for instance, TFSI anions, CF3SO2N(−)SO2CF3. Variable temperature 19F SSNMR measurements was carried out on LiTFSI-PEO and Al2O3-PEG9-anion (TFSI− grafted on the surface of Al2O3) in PEO/PEGDME. The linewidths fitting of the 19F NMR signals showed that the linewidths of the two systems have obvious differences within the whole temperature range (Fig. 2c) [54]. The NMR linewidths of the 19F NMR signal in the LiTFSI-PEO sample have changed drastically with the increasing temperature, indicating that the mobility of the CF3 groups in TFSI− is correlated to the mobility of the PEO matrix. At high temperatures, the PEO chains become more flexible, which greatly increases the freedom of movement of the TFSI−; and its molecular tumbling averages out the anisotropic interactions, resulting in an intense SSNMR signal narrowing transition. In contrast, the 19F linewidth behaves less temperature dependent in the Al2O3-PEG9-anion system, suggesting the flexibility/mobility of the PEO matrix has a negligible effect on the local mobility of anionic molecules grafted to the Al2O3 surface.

SSNMR techniques provide important information about ionic dynamics at different time scales in CSEs by measuring spin-lattice relaxation time in the laboratory frame of reference (T 1), T 2, and T 1ρ [35,55]. Greenbaum et al. studied the local environment and dynamics of Li and F in PEO/SiO2 CSEs by measuring T 1 as a function of temperature (Fig. 2d) [56]. The 7Li T 1 measurement shows very broad minimum values for both filler-free and SiO2-containing polymers. The broad T 1 minimum represents the distribution of correlation time, indicating heterogeneity of the local environment, which is characteristic of an amorphous system. The T 1 data above the minimum value can be used to extract the activation energy of short-range Li+ motion or provide an estimation of the timescale of motion [57]. The 19F T 1 ’s of the CF3 and CF2 environments in the two SEs presented similar temperature dependence. The 19F T 1 ’s of CF3 in both SEs are notably longer than CF2, which can be attributed to the rapid rotation of the CF3 group. The 19F T 1 of the SE containing SiO2 is slightly longer, which is also associated with the decrease of linewidth, indicating that adding SiO2 to the polymer electrolyte is beneficial for increasing the mobility of anions. Yao et al. measured the 1H T 2 relaxation of nano SiO2/PEO CSEs by 1H→13C CP. It was found that as the content of nano-SiO2 increases, the crystallinity of PEO decreases, and the mobility of PEO chains in the amorphous region of PEO decreases sharply. This is mainly due to the hydrogen bond interaction between nano-SiO2 and PEO [58].

A typical example of employing two-dimensional exchange spectroscopy (2D-EXSY) NMR to probe slow molecular motion or chemical exchange process (with correlation times of milliseconds to seconds) is given in Fig. 2e. Zhang et al. [59] characterized the Li-ion exchange processes existing between PEO: LiTFSI polymer and LLZO ceramic by 6Li 2D -EXSY NMR using a PEO: LiTFSI + 10 vol% LLZO CSEs. LiOH is detected in the composite material; and an exchange process involving the three Li-containing components (PEO, LLZO, and LiOH) was confirmed. This result suggests that slow exchange between ceramic and polymer at the time scale of seconds is possible via the help of LiOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ion migration pathway in organic-inorganic CSEs
ce:section.ce:para: The Li+ conductivity of the organic-inorganic CSEs is a critical factor for stable cycling and high performance of all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. Therefore, exploring the Li+ conduction mechanism of organic-inorganic CSEs is important for effectively developing high-performance solid electrolytes. CP MAS NMR can be used to probe the dipolar coupling interactions between Li+ and proton in CSEs, which strongly depends on Li–H distance. In addition to CP experiments, spin-echo double resonance (SEDOR), rotation-echo double resonance (REDOR), and transferred echo double resonance (TEDOR) are all double-resonance experiments using heteronuclear dipole coupling to explore the spatial correlation information. Moreover, a second spectral dimension is usually introduced helping to understand the one-dimensional signals better through chemical bonding or spatial connectivity, and even multiple quantum signals that are inaccessible in one-dimensional spectra. For instance, one-dimensional CPMAS NMR can only give the sum of all the resonances from all the different sites; 2D heteronuclear correlation (HETCOR) NMR spectroscopy can be employed to separate the signals from each distinct site. 2D-EXSY is often used to evaluate whether the species at different chemical environments have undergone chemical exchange at the interface of organic-inorganic CSEs or cathode/anode and SSEs and further to quantify the exchange rate/jump rate. In this section, we briefly describe the progress of using SSNMR techniques to investigate the Li+ conductive mechanism of organic-inorganic CSEs in recent years.

Hu and co-workers used the high-resolution 6Li SSNMR spectra of PEO-LLZO CSEs to distinguish the local environments of Li+ residents in the PEO polymer, LLZO ceramic phase, and the LLZO/PEO interface [28]. The transport channel of Li+ in CSEs can be determined by the selective isotope labeling method. The assembled 6Li | CSEs | 6Li symmetric batteries can be cycled under a certain current density. Upon batteries cycling, the 6Li ions are stripped from one electrode and moved to the other electrode. When the 6Li ions pass through the CSEs, they will replace some of the 7Li ions, resulting in the enrichment of 6Li and the scarcity of 7Li ions. The transport path of Li+ can be revealed by comparing the 6Li and 7Li SSNMR spectra of the original and the cycled CSEs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the 6Li SSNMR spectrum of the cycled CSEs had a significantly increased intensity at the LLZO peak, and only a slight increase of the interfacial Li and PEO (LiClO4) resonances compared with that of the original LLZO-PEO (LiClO4) electrolytes. This indicates that Li ions mainly pass through the LLZO ceramic particles and minimize the fraction of interface or the PEO polymer phase in the LLZO-PEO (LiClO4) electrolytes [28]. The 7Li 2D EXSY spectrum shown in Fig. 3b provides information about the ion exchange among different Li components. Two diagonal peaks have resulted from LLZO and PEO (LiClO4). The circled region between LLZO and PEO (LiClO4) peaks on the diagonal is from interfacial Li. To observe the ion exchange process more clearly, 1D cross-sections were extracted at the chemical shift of LiClO4 from the 2D EXSY spectra acquired with different mixing times. With a very short mixing time (0.0001 s), only LiClO4 peaks are observed. As the mixing time increases, the 6Li signal of the interface at 1.4 ppm gradually increases, indicating ion exchange between PEO (LiClO4) and the interfacial Li. No direct exchange between PEO (LiClO4) and LLZO was observed due to the comparatively large distance apart. Similar methods were adopted in a further study to investigate the effect of plasticizers and different proportions of LLZO fillers on the ion transport path in the LLZO-PEO (LiTFSI) system [60]. As the proportion of LLZO ceramic increases, the ion mobility decreases, and the Li+ migration path gradually transitions from the polymer to the LLZO ceramic phase. When the liquid TEGDME additive is added, the ionic conductivity of the polymer can be significantly improved, and the ion transport path returns to the polymer phase (Fig. 3c).

1H→6Li CP NMR spectra as a function of contact time were acquired to directly observe the interfacial lithium ions in LLZO - PEO (LiClO4) - TEGDME [61]. As shown in Fig. 3d, there are two resonances, one at 2.4 ppm from the ceramic filler LLZO, and the other at 1.8 ppm from the PEO/LLZO interface. The intensities of the two resonances increase with the increasing CP contact time. The interface/LLZO resonance peak ratio can be obtained as 0.4: 1 through fitting the NMR spectrum with a contact time of 6 ms. This ratio is much higher than that of 6Li direct polarization NMR (0.2:1), indicating that the interfacial Li+ is closer to PEO than LLZO and hence can be selectively enhanced by the protons in PEO. The ionic conductivity of the solid electrolyte decreased significantly in the first two days. Therefore, the authors studied the changes of lithium ions in different components with aging time by 6Li NMR (Fig. 3e). The 6Li NMR spectra of LLZO-PEO (LiClO4)-TEGDME composite with different aging times showed that the LLZO phase resonance decreased slightly and the signal at the interface increased upon aging. This suggests that a small fraction of the Li in LLZO is converted to the interface Li, possibly due to gradual interaction between TEGDME and LLZO over time. In addition, the signal of LiClO4 gradually shifted to the right, which means that the Li salt was dissociated from the PEO/TEGDME complex and re-dissolved in pure TEGDME. Since Li conduction is mainly carried out through the Li salt in the PEO/TEGDME complex, LiClO4 dissociated from PEO/TEGDME is expected to reduce the conductivity. The distribution of LiClO4 in TEGDME and PEO/TEGDME complexes will eventually reach equilibrium, so the conductivity decreases first and then reaches a fixed value over time.

To fulfill or enhance the direct Li+ exchange between polymer and ceramic, Guo et al. [62] prepared LLZTO nanoparticle coated by the thin film of PAN (Fig. 3f), a conjugated polymer containing C = C bonds, and investigated by 2D 1H→7Li HETCOR and 7Li 2D EXSY. The 2D 1H→7Li HETCOR spectrum shows that the 7Li signal in LLZTO has a significant correlation with the two 1H signals in PAN, while the 7Li signal of LiTFSI is only correlated to the 1H signal associated with the conjugate structure (Fig. 3g). The 7Li 2D EXSY spectrum shows a clear cross peak between 1.52 ppm (LLZTO) and 0.12 ppm (LiTFSI on PAN) signals, suggesting direct Li+ exchange between LLZTO ceramic and PAN polymer is possible. The above results prove that the uniform conjugated polymer nano-coating formed on the surface of the ceramic oxide particles can provide a channel for Li+ conduction between ceramic and polymer.

Garnet-type ion conductors are suitable candidates due to their high ionic conductivity, well mechanical properties, and wide electrochemical window. However, it has been reported that garnets are unstable in ambient air, and Li2CO3/LiOH impurities are easily formed on the surface [63–65]. The introduction of perovskite and NASICON-type (e.g., Li1.5Al0.5Ti1.5P3O12) ionic conductors into CSEs have been demonstrated beneficial to improve the ionic conductivity and air stability of CSEs [66–68].

Li and co-workers [69] used 6Li→7Li tracer-exchange NMR techniques to determine the Li+ distribution and conduction mechanism in the PEO-based NASICON-LiZr2(PO4)3 CSEs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the asymmetric 6Li NMR signal shows that the composite electrolyte is composed of two Li+ environments, one is Li+ closely bounded to the EO unit in PEO, and the other is Li+ in a disordered local environment. With the addition of Al2O3 and LiZr2(PO4)3 to the PEO-LiTFSI matrix, more Li+ migrates from the regions near PEO to the disordered environment. And the higher concentration of LiTFSI in the electrolyte, the stronger the signal strength of Li+ in the disordered environment (left and center of Fig. 4a). Therefore, the distribution of Li+ in two local environments in CSEs depends on both the amount of ceramic and the Li-salt concentration. The Li+ transport pathway was identified using 6Li→7Li tracer-exchange NMR (right of Fig. 4a). The results show that 6Li ions prefer to transport through the disordered Li+ environment as the 6Li signal is enriched by 6.4%, whereas the right peak decreases from 39.8% to 33.4%. Therefore, the CSEs with more Li+ resided in the disordered environment are expected to obtain higher ionic conductivity [70]. Fig. 4b shows the 7Li NMR spectra of poly(ethylene oxide)/Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 (PEO/LATP) 70/30 (left) and LATP/SPE 70/30 (right) in the temperature range of 25–75 °C. The room temperature spectrum of LATP/PEO 70/30 is perfectly superimposed with the room temperature spectrum of LATP powder. Peak deconvolution of the quantitative 7Li NMR demonstrates that the peaks at −1.35 ppm and −0.61 ppm correspond to two lithium sites (peak 1 and peak 2) contributing to the high mobility of lithium ions in these phases (Fig. 4c). With temperature increasing, peak 2 gradually disappeared due to the fast exchange at the time scale of ∼ 0.1 s, indicating a mobile ionic character of the Li+ ions. The spectrum of LATP/SPE 70/30 contains a third contribution (peak 3) due to the presence of the lithium salt in the polymer, accounting for about 6% of the total lithium. By increasing the temperature, peak 2 progressively disappears; however, the contributions of peak 3 from lithium salt in the polymer and peak 1 from the ceramic phase do not merge at all, even at the highest investigated temperature. This is presumably because the size of the LATP particles is too large to allow a complete exchange of lithium at the NMR time scale. No evidence shows the ceramic-polymer interface has any contribution to the conduction of lithium.

Compared with inorganic oxide electrolytes, sulfides have higher ionic conductivity and lower grain boundary resistance. Hu et al. [71] performed 6Li→7Li tracer exchange and 6Li NMR to analyze the ion conduction mechanism of the Li10GeP2S12 (LGPS)/PEO (LiTFSI) composites (Fig. 4d). The total amount of 6Li signal integration of LGPS-PEO interfaces is increased by nearly 3-fold after cycling, and PEO (LiTFSI) is increased by a factor of 1.4, indicating that Li+ ion transport mainly occurs in the LGPS-PEO interfaces. The decrease of the 6Li content in the bulk LGPS may be due to the decomposition of bulk LGPS during electrochemical cycling. Hence, the transport of lithium ions takes place mainly at the LGPS-PEO interface; while a small amount of PEO (LiTFSI) also contributes to the ion conduction.

SSNMR technique is a non-destructive and highly selective characterization tool for materials, which is widely adopted in structural analysis and monitoring the kinetic chemical processes. Compared with other characterization techniques (XRD, XPS, SEM, TEM, EIS), the SSNMR spectroscopy is relatively expensive and requires a relatively long learning curve, which therefore has not yet been widely popularized. In addition, from the practical application aspects, it is necessary to characterize and understand the real diffusion patterns of lithium ions in the CSEs through in-situ NMR with adequate resolution. At present, in-situ solid-state NMR experiments can only be performed under static conditions. Hence, combining the MAS rotation with in situ data acquisition is challenging but of great value for the actual understanding of the Li+ ion transmission process. In recent years, organic-inorganic CSEs have attracted widespread public attention due to the combination of the advantages of polymers and high ionic conductivity oxides. In the review, the latest research progress of SSNMR on CSEs are briefly introduced, discussing the interactions between polymer chains and Li+ cations, the structure of organic components, the ion dynamics, and the ion conduction mechanism. We hope that this review will promote broader research interest in SSNMR, as well as a deeper understanding of how to use the SSNMR technique to explore the ion conduction mechanisms and spatial local environment in organic-inorganic CSEs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xueyan Zhang received a Master’s degree from Xinjiang University in 2019. Currently, she is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Electrochemistry at Harbin Institute of Technology, China. Her research interests focus on the preparation of composite solid electrolytes and the application of solid-state NMR techniques in the interface of organic/inorganic electrolytes.

Hua Huo obtained her Ph.D. in Chemistry in 2009 from the State University of New York at Stony Brook. After an ALiSTORE-ERI post-doctoral fellowship at ICMCB-CNRS and University of Cambridge, in 2013, she joined Harbin Institute of Technology. Currently, she is an associate professor at HIT. Her research focuses on solid-state NMR applications in energy-related materials (such as zeolitic materials, cathodes and anodes for LIBs/NIBs, solid electrolytes).
